rationale_for_baseline_tier: Irritant napkin dermatitis is benign and managed at home with barrier care and irritant avoidance, with routine child-health/GP review (T1). Because this is an infant feeding less, an explicit dehydration safety-net to ED (T4) is mandatory; secondary infection routes to prompt review.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Fewer than one wet nappy in 8 hours, floppy/lethargic or hard to wake, sunken soft spot, persistent vomiting, or refusing all feeds
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Attend ED promptly (000 if very floppy/unresponsive)
escalation_edges.rationale: Signs of significant infant dehydration needing assessment ± IV fluids.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he has fewer than one wet nappy in 8 hours, becomes floppy or hard to wake, the soft spot on his head sinks, he vomits repeatedly, or refuses all feeds — take him to the Emergency Department promptly (call 000 if he's very floppy or unresponsive).

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Blisters, pustules, honey-crusting, hot spreading redness, or fever
escalation_edges.rationale: Secondary candidal/bacterial infection or cellulitis needing specific treatment.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Infant — rapid dehydration risk and mandatory weight-based dosing
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lower threshold for review; defer analgesic dosing to weight-based in-person prescribing.
Give clear, reassuring, practical barrier-care steps and explicit dehydration red flags.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Stop the fragranced wipes, clean with lukewarm water and cotton, apply the zinc barrier paste thickly at every change, and give nappy-free air time. The rash should improve over a few days. Keep offering feeds and watch his wet nappies.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Barrier care + GP/child-health-nurse review in 2–3 days; ED for dehydration; prompt GP for secondary infection
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: ED if he becomes floppy, has few wet nappies, vomits repeatedly, or develops a fever.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Irritant napkin dermatitis (fold-sparing) post-gastroenteritis; candidal/bacterial and dehydration screens negative; barrier care; analgesic dosing deferred to weight-based in-person prescribing.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating the rash with no infant dehydration safety-net.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: A poorly-feeding infant becomes significantly dehydrated and presents late.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Not screening candidal/bacterial superinfection or feeding/hydration.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: ED/ambulance for a benign fold-sparing napkin rash in a well-hydrated infant — unnecessary.
